coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots from waste cellulose diacetate as a sensor for tetracycline detection and fluorescence ink
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ziming
Guo, Yanzhu
Zhang, Tao
Ma, Jiliang
Li, Haiming
Zhou, Jinghui
Wang, Zhiwei
Sun, Runcang
coredata.dc:description: As one type of the solid wastes, the increasing contamination of waste cellulose diacetate (CDA) from discarded cigarette filters is a growing problem worldwide. Finding a facile and suitable approach to convert the CDA into value-added materials is of significance. Herein, we reported a green, simple and effective method to reuse CDA as precursor for preparing fluorescence N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via one-pot hydrothermal carbonization in aqueous solution with low-cost ammonium hydroxide as the passivation agent. The N-CDs showed a quantum yield up to 22.4% with a maximum emission at 415 nm and excitation at 320 nm. Interestingly, the N-CDs exhibited high selectivity toward tetracycline (TC) as their fluorescence was obviously quenched by TC as a result of inner-filter effect. A linear relationship was fabricated over concentration range of 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 0.06 μM. Moreover, the N-CDs could also be applied as fluorescent ink for anti-forgery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose acetate
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal carbonization
Tetracycline
Fluorescence ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Imaging with the fluorescent ink of N-CDs
FL properties of the synthesized N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program ( XLYC1807232 ), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control ( 2019KF30 ), Liaoning Baiqianwan Talents Program and the National Natural Science Foundation ( 31500493 ).

The work was supported by the Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1807232), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (2019KF30), Liaoning Baiqianwan Talents Program and the National Natural Science Foundation (31500493).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of preparation route of the N-CDs.
The effect of mass ratio of ammonium hydroxide to CDA on the QY of N-CDs (a); TEM image (b); HRTEM image (c); size distribution (d); XRD pattern (e) and Raman spectrum of the N-CDs (f).
FTIR spectra (a) and XPS survey scans (b) of the N-CDs and CDA; High-resolution S2p (c), N1s (d), C1s (e) and O1s (f) spectra of N-CDs; High-resolution C1s (g), O1s (h) of CDA.

UV–vis absorption (Abs), optimal excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) PL spectra of N-CDs aqueous solution, inset of are the images of the N-CDs under sun light and UV light (a); PL emission spectra of N-CDs at varying excitation wavelengths in the range of 310 nm to 420 nm (b); FL intensity (c) and spectra (d) of N-CDs in aqueous solutions with different pH values; FL spectra of N-CD sunder different irradiation durations (e); The Msc cell viability of N-CDs with different concentrations (f).

FL emission spectra of the N-CDs upon addition of TC with various concentrations (0–240 μM) (a); the F/F0 values versus concentrations of TC and the inset are the linear relationship between the F/F0 values and concentrations of TC in the range of 0–80 μM (b).

Selectivity of N-CDs toward TC (a); the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of other antibiotic, amino acid or metal ions (b), all the concentrations of the other antibiotic, amino acid and metal ions were constant at 100 μM.

UV–Vis adsorption spectrum of TC and excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (a); UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC, N-CDs and N-CDs/TC solutions (b); fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs before (c)and after addition of TC (d).
Photographic images of papers with hand-written characters using N-CDs as a fluorescent ink under day light (a), UV light (b) and both of them (c).
Comparison of the presented platform with other reported platforms for TC detection.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots from waste cellulose diacetate as a sensor for tetracycline detection and fluorescence ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, School of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, School of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, 1#Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one type of the solid wastes, the increasing contamination of waste cellulose diacetate (CDA) from discarded cigarette filters is a growing problem worldwide. Finding a facile and suitable approach to convert the CDA into value-added materials is of significance. Herein, we reported a green, simple and effective method to reuse CDA as precursor for preparing fluorescence N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) via one-pot hydrothermal carbonization in aqueous solution with low-cost ammonium hydroxide as the passivation agent. The N-CDs showed a quantum yield up to 22.4% with a maximum emission at 415 nm and excitation at 320 nm. Interestingly, the N-CDs exhibited high selectivity toward tetracycline (TC) as their fluorescence was obviously quenched by TC as a result of inner-filter effect. A linear relationship was fabricated over concentration range of 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 0.06 μM. Moreover, the N-CDs could also be applied as fluorescent ink for anti-forgery.

ce:section.ce:para: As a type of broad-spectrum and effective antibiotics, tetracycline (TC) can kill gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [1], and thus is widely used in aquacultural and veterinary medicines with the aim of preventing or treating infections [2]. However, widespread use of TC leads its accumulation in animals, which will migrate into foods, e.g., milk and meat, and thus accumulates in human body, eventually enhance the risk to develop various diseases, e.g. tetracycline pigmentation teeth [3], glossitis [4], angular stomatitis, and liver damage [5]. Therefore, monitoring the TC in animal or medicine products at any moments is vital. However, the traditional analytical methods, e.g., high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], colorimetric analysis and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [8–10], are facing some problems, including sophisticated and expensive instruments, complicated and time-consuming processes, while the fluorescence spectrophotometry can avoid the above disadvantages and exhibits the merit of facile manipulation. Currently, various types of nanomaterials have been fabricated and applied as probes in the fluorescence spectrophotometry method, upon which the accurate determination of TC will be altered. However, the traditionally used fluorescent materials, e.g., perovskite nanocrystals [11], organic fluorescent dyes [12], metallic quantum dot [13], are usually fairly toxic. Therefore, developing the fluorescent materials with excellent sensitivity, low toxicity and selectivity for distinguishing and assaying of TC is urgent and necessary. As a novel type of nanostructured carbons, carbon dots (CDs) with sizes less than 10 nm [14], have gained abundant attentions as the functional fluorescent probes [15–17]. CDs display excellent fluorescence (FL) properties and offer unique advantages over other fluorescent agents owing to their favorable properties, e.g., cheap, non-toxicity, biocompatible [18,19]. The most popular view at the moment is that CDs can be regarded as small molecules or polymers after condensation, crosslinking and carbonization [20]. Therefore, CDs usually possess the structure of both fluorescent polymers and inorganic carbon core [16]. This unique structure enables the CDs with excellent water solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, extensive literatures have been reported various approaches for preparing the CDs with the desired potential applications of CDs in chemical catalysis [21], sensing and bioimaging [22,23]. Among all of the approaches, the hydrothermal treatment of biomass-based precursors, including starch, chitosan, cellulose, lignin and hemicellulose, has caught our attentions due to their renewable, cheap and green properties. However, the above biomass-based precursors generally have their own value and usually applied as sources in industrial fields. Therefore, finding the discarded materials as precursors to generate high-quality CDs instead of the valuable industrial materials remains urgent desirable.

CDA is the main component in discarded cigarette filters, which are considered as one type of the solid wastes on the earth. It has been reported that almost 767,000 metric tons of cigarette filters are deposited into the earth every year, which leads to the waste of resources [24]. Furthermore, the discarded CDA is non-biodegradable polymer [25,26], which would generate serious pollutions to our ocean, groundwater and soil. It should be noted that the discarded CDA were probably used as precursors to prepare CDs, since it is a type of cellulose-based derivatives. Up to now, cellulose or its derivatives have been successfully applied as raw materials to prepare CDs as reported in lots of literatures [27,28]. To date, the CDA has a lower crystallization structure than cellulose and thus possesses a higher accessibility during reaction [25], which would be beneficial to generate the CDs with high yield. In addition, the discarded CDA usually contain a controlled amount of contaminants on their surfaces, e.g., nicotine and tar [29–31], adsorbed cigarette smoke particles [32–34], and residues of tobacco [35], which contain some elements, e.g., sulfur and nitrogen [34]. The contaminates would also take participated into the formation of the CDs and impart some elements, e.g., sulfur, nitrogen, onto the CDs, and thus beneficial to attain the CDs with high quantum yield.

The aim of this paper was to find a facile and green approach to prepare high quality CDs from discarded CDA. Herein, one-pot hydrothermal method with the properties of low cost, environmentally friendly and easily large-scale producing was employed into the process of preparing CDs. In order to enhance the quantum yield of CDs, the nitrogen element was introduced into the surfaces of CDs, using ammonium hydroxide as the passivation agent rather than using any expensive and poisonous amine-terminated passivation agent. The derived N-CDs could serve as a selective and sensitive fluorescence sensor for “on-off” detection of TC in complex environmental conditions. Moreover, owing to their high quantum yield and bright fluorescence properties, the N-CDs could be used as fluorescent anti-fake ink. The findings of this work would not only give highlights to exploit the discarded CDA as valuable materials, but also provide low-cost and green biomass-based materials for generating high quality CDs.

Ammonium hydroxide, TC, amoxicillin (AMO), clarithromycin (CLA), roxithromycin (ROX), penicillin (PC), gentamicin (GEN), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), methionine (Met), lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Try), threonine (Thr), aspartic acid (Asp) and valine (Val), were provided by Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The metal ions used for the selectivity test were in form of chlorinated salt, and all of them were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) as well. The other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without pretreatment. The deionized water was used throughout the entire experimental process.

The N-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method. After washing with water for several times, the cigarette filters were cut into small segments, and then dried in oven at 80 °C for 1 h as a preliminary. Approximately, 0.2 g pretreated cigarette filters were added into ammonium hydroxide solution and the mixtures were then diluted with deionized water to 20 mL. Subsequently, the mixtures were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave with a Teflon-line inner chamber and heated at 240 °C for 15 h. After the reaction, the auto clave was cooled to room temperature. The mixtures were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the black precipitates. After that, the supernatant solution was passed through a 0.22 μm micron filter and dialyzed against deionized water in a dialysis tube (MWCO 1000) for 24 h with the aim of removing impurities. The purified N-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

All photoluminescence spectra were obtained at room temperature on a F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a voltage of 400 V. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs was obtained at room temperature on a UV1006M031 UV–vis spectrophotometer (VARIAN, America). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of cigarette filters and N-CDs were observed with a Frontier spectrophotometer (Frontier I, PerkinElmer, America). The morphology and size of the N-CDs was performed on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a 6100 X-ray diffractometer with the Cu Kα as radiation source (40 kV, 30 mA) in the range of 10° to 60° (Shimadzu, Japan). Raman spectroscopy of the N-CDs was characterized by a LabRAM ARAMIS Raman spectroscope equipped with a 1064 nm laser source (HORIBA Scientific Jobin, France). All X-ray photoelectron (XPS) measurements were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America).

The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was determined using quinine sulfate with a 54% QY in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution as the standards at 360 nm excitation wavelength according to the following equation.
(1)
QY =
I /
I r
×
A r
/ A
×
n 2
/
n r 2
where the subscript r denotes the standard samples. Q and I is the quantum yield and the measured emission intensity, while n and A separately refers to the refractive index of the solvent and the absorbance.

The cell toxicity of N-CDs was measured using the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Mouse skin cells (Msc) were grown in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) solution on a 96-well plate (1.5 × 104 cells and 100 μL DMEM per well) at 24.3 °C in the incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Subsequently, the DMEM solution was replaced by the N-CDs with various concentrations (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 μg mL−1) and the cells were then incubated for another 24 h at 24.3 °C with 5% CO2. Each sample was repetitive execution in triplicate. After incubation, the DMEM solution was removed and the CCK-8 was added into the well, which were followed by incubation at 37 °C for 4 h with 5% CO2. Finally, the optical density of the cell was measured at 490 nm. The Msc without N-CDs was incubated under the same conditions and used as controlling samples. The cytotoxicity of the N-CDs was represented by the viability of the mouse skin cells, which was calculated according to the following equation.
(2)
Msc
viability
%
=
N D
/
N T
× 100 %
where, NT and ND are the absorbance of the controlling samples and the Msc cells with various concentrations of N-CDs, respectively.

The detection of TC using N-CDs was performed at room temperature in deionized water. Approximately, 200 μL N-CDs (3.5 mg L−1) solution and 5 mL Na2HPO4 buffer (200 μM) solution were added into a 50 mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, 100 μL TC aqueous solutions with various concentrations (0.25 mM–12.00 mM) were injected into the flask and the solutions were diluted to 50 mL with deionized water. The solutions were then incubated for 5 min at room temperature and then 5 mL solutions were took out and analyzed on a F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The emission spectrum was recorded at 320 nm excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 600 nm. For assessing the selectivity of TC detection, N-CDs was added into the solutions of following substances, e.g., AMO, CLA, ROX, GEN, PC, Cys, Phe, Met, Lys, Tyr, Leu, Try, Thr, Asp, Val, Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, under the same conditions. Finally, all of the above solutions were analyzed using the same procedure as analyzing TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging with the fluorescent ink of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A fountain pen loaded with the N-CDs solution was used to draw some words on an unbleached paper and then the photos of the papers were took under the sunlight or UV light at 365 nm.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the N-CDs were synthesized from recycled CDA via one-pot hydrothermal treatment, which was performed at 240 °C for 15 h. The dilute ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution was applied simultaneously as reaction medium and the passivation agent instead of employing other expensive or toxic organic passivation agents, which usually contained lots of terminal amine groups. In order to obtain the N-CDs with the highest QY, the effect of mass ratio of ammonium hydroxide to CDA on the QY of N-CDs was evaluated and the results are shown in Fig. 2a. The QY value of the N-CDs was increased from 8.3% to 22.4% with increasing the mass ratio of ammonium hydroxide to CDA from 0:1 to 10:1. Further elevating of the mass ratio to 15:1 would generate a decrease in the QY of the N-CDs. Therefore, the mass ratio of ammonium hydroxide to CDA was optimized to 10:1, which would generate the N-CDs with a QY value up to 22.4% and this value was comparable to those values from other biomass-based precursors.

According to the previous publications on degradation and carbonization of cellulose [36–40], the possibly formation mechanism of N-CDs was considered to be involving in the hydrolysis of CDA in the first step, followed by the condensation of small segments to produce N-CDs, which is illustrated in Fig. 1. CDA is a type of cellulose derivative and composed by the glucose-like units, which was linked by the glycosidic bond [41]. In the first step, the reaction of CDA in the ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution would lead to the intermediated CDA fragments, e.g., glucose, fructose, and acetic acid, through cleavage of the glycosidic bond and the deacetylation [42]. Additionally, novel kinds of oxygen, nitrogen containing groups were probably generated under the presence of the ammonium hydroxide. The above mentioned CDA fragments were furtherly condensed and carbonized in the following hydrothermal treatment, which generate the final carbon structures of N-CDs [43]. The success passivation of nitrogen on the surface of N-CDs was through the nucleophilic substitution of ammonia to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon [16].

As shown in Fig. 2b, the N-CDs were well-dispersed spherical particles. It can be observed from the histogram that the sizes of N-CDs were ranged from 1 nm to 3 nm with an average diameter of 1.93 nm. The HRTEM image of N-CDs is shown in Fig. 2c. The inset in Fig. 2c clearly showed that there was no lattice in the structure of the N-CDs. The similar phenomenon was reported in the previous literatures [44,45]. Additionally, the CDA showed two broad peaks at 9.7° and 18.2° in its XRD pattern (Fig. 2d), which are ascribed to the typical crystalline phase of the CDA whereas the CDs only had a wide peak at 22.3°, which is attributed to the amorphous phase of carbons in the N-CDs [46,47]. The Raman spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 2e exhibited the typical of D and G bands at 1353 cm−1 and 1532 cm−1, which are corresponding to disorder-induced sp3 carbon atoms and E2g vibration mode of sp2 bonded carbons, respectively. The relative intensity of the typical bands (ID/IG) of N-CDs was 1.07, indicating that the N-CDs contained both sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and sp2 surface defects [48,49].

Surface functional groups of N-CDs were identified by FTIR and XPS techniques. As shown in Fig. 3a, the FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited three peaks at 3265, 1429 and 775 cm−1, which are assigned to stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H, stretching vibrations of CN and bending vibrations of NH, respectively. The presence of these peaks confirmed that the N element was successfully doped into the N-CDs sample [47]. The peak at 1116 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of SO3/C-O, indicated the presence of sulfur element in the N-CDs [21,41]. In addition, the XPS technique was used to confirm the surface chemical compositions of N-CDs and CDA. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3b) exhibited three strong peaks at 284.76 eV (C1s), 531.35 eV (O1s), 399.64 eV (N1s) and one small peak at 167.83 eV (S2p), whereas the spectrum of CDA displayed two peaks at 284.81 eV (C1s) and 532.6 eV (O1s). The signals for the N1s and S2p were not found in the spectrum of CDA due to their small contents. The nitrogen and sulfur atoms contents in the N-CDs were 13.27% and 1.49%, respectively and both of them were higher than the corresponding values in the CDA, further confirming that the nitrogen and sulfur elements were successfully doped into the N-CDs. The sulfur element of N-CDs was originated from CDA, due to no additional sulfur element containing substances, except for CDA, were used during the whole procedure of preparing N-CDs, while the nitrogen element in N-CDs was mainly came from the passivator of ammonium hydroxide and carbon source of CDA. As shown in Fig. 3c, the S2p high-resolution spectra of N-CDs proved three peaks at 167.47, 168.06 and 168.88 eV, which are attributed to the C-S(O)2-C, C-S(O)3-C and C-S(O)4-C bonds, respectively. The peaks at 399.04, 399.74 and 400.66 eV, corresponding to the CN, CN and NH bonds, were clearly observed in the N1s high-resolution spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 3d). The C1s high-resolution spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3e) exhibited five different peaks at 284.5 eV (CC), 285.2 eV (C=C), 285.9 eV (C-N/C-S), 285.9 eV (CO) and 287.9 eV (C=O). The high resolution C1s spectra of CDA (Fig. 3g) gave three peaks at 284.8 eV (CC), 286.5 eV (C-O/C-N) and 289.1 eV (C=O). As shown in Fig. 3f and h, the peaks at 530.61/531.28/53.24 eV and 531.82/532.54/533.33 eV could be clearly observed in the O1s high-resolution spectra of the N-CDs and CDA samples, which are attributed to CO, C-O-C and C-OH groups, respectively. These results demonstrated that the N-CDs were nano-sized particles rich in carbon-rich with graphite-like structure and inherited amounts of oxygenous, amine and sulfonic groups on their surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL properties of the synthesized N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited excellent optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the N-CDs aqueous solution was yellow wish under day light and could emit bright blue luminescence under irradiation with a 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs exhibited two peaks at 270 nm and 300 nm, which are assigned to CC bonds (π = π* transition) and CO bonds (n = π*), respectively [50,51]. The optimal emission wavelength of N-CDs was centered at 415 nm with the excitation of 320 nm. The corresponding QY was determined to be 22.4% using quinine sulfate as reference [52–54]. Furthermore, the peaks in the emission spectra of N-CDs was red shifted from 410 nm to 480 nm and their intensities were gradually decreased when the excitation wavelength was enhanced from 300 nm to 350 nm with a 10 nm increament (Fig. 4b). This excitation-dependent FL behavior was widely reported in previous literatures [22,55]. The fluorescent stability of the N-CDs in water was evaluated by measuring the changes in their FL intensities upon affecting by pH values of aqueous solution and the irradiation durations and the results are shown in Fig. 4c, d and e. It was observed that the N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent stability in weak acidic aqueous solutions with a pH value in the range of 4 to 7 and the strongest FL intensity of the N-CDs was obtained when the pH value of aqueous solution was 5. Both increasing pH value from 7 to 13 and decreasing pH value from 4 to 1 would obviously reduce the FL intensity of the N-CDs, which was probably attributed to protonation or deprotonation of functional groups on their surfaces [56]. Thus, it was assumed that the N-CDs were stable in the slightly acidic environment. It should be noted that the emission peak of the N-CDs was gradually blue-shifted with enhancing the pH value from 1 to 13. This pH-dependent fluorescence emission change behavior was also observed in previously reported graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide [57], nitrogen-doped carbon dots from citric acid [58] and from cellulose [59]. Some possible mechanism models responsive to pH, e.g., protonation and deprotonation, change of energy level, self-aggregation, protective shell and aggregation of carbon dots, had been proposed based on the previous literatures [60,61]. However, a distinct mechanism for this behavior had not been established and approved yet [62], due to the infancy research into carbon dots, e.g., no well-defined and precise structures of carbon dots have been confirmed so far, which would restrict more exactly analysis in the relationships between their structures and optical properties [63]. Furthermore, it was hard to get a conclusion on this issue as the carbon dots, prepared from the same precursors or though same procedures, might exhibit different pH-dependent fluorescence emission change behavior. For example, Yuan had reported the carbons dots from graphite rods were with a red-shifted fluorescence emission change behavior [64], while Fan prepared the carbon dots using the same precursors but were with a blue-shifted behavior [65]. Liu [66] and Barati [67] prepared the carbon dots through the same hydrothermal procedure from citric acid and glucose, respectively. However, these two types of carbon dots exhibited different pH-dependent fluorescence emission behavior. Furthermore, the N-CDs exhibited excellent photo-stability as convinced by their scarcely changed FL intensities upon irradiating from 10 to 120 min, which was probably attributed to the present of carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs [68].

The toxicity of N-CDs was also carried out against MSc by MTT assay, which was incubated in DMEM upon addition of N-CDs with various concentrations for 24 h and the results are shown in Fig. 4f. It was found that the cell viability of Msc was still higher than 90% when the concentration of N-CDs was up to 150 μg mL−1. This result indicated that the N-CDs was non-toxic to normal cells and could be used as a suitable optical probe to detect TC in possible practical biological environment.

In this study, a fluorescent turn-off system to detect TC was fabricated using the N-CDs as their fluorescence could be easily quenched by TC without any chemical treatment. As the N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent stability in the pH range of 5 to 7, the PBS buffer solution with a pH value of 7 was selected as the solvent for the TC detection in the following experiment. The quenching efficiency (F/F0) was calculated on the basis of the fluorescence of the N-CDs solution in the absence and presence of TC, which was denoted by F and F0, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra and quenching efficiencies of N-CDs solutions upon addition of TC with various concentrations are shown in Fig. 5b, respectively. It was found that the maximum intensity in the emission spectra of N-CDs was gradually reduced upon of TC with increased concentrations from 0 μM to 240 μM. A good linear regression correlation between the quenching efficiency and the TC concentration in the range of 0–80 μM was observed (depicted in the inset of Fig. 5b), and the correlation could be described with the equation of F 0/F = 100.9513 − 8.1248 × C
TC (μM) with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9924. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.06 μM according to 3σ rule, which was comparable to the values using other reported CDs [12,50,52,69,70]. As shown in Table 1 , a comparison was made between other currently available methods and the presented method in this work to detect TC. When compared with other reported methods for detection of TC, the presented N-CDs from CDA exhibited wider linear range (0–80 μM) and relatively low LOD (0.06 μM). The results confirmed that the accuracy of proposed fluorescence method relying on N-CDs in this work were close or better than that of other methods or fluorescence method using other carbon dots reported in the previous literatures.

In order to analyze the selectively detection of N-CDs toward TC, other substances might present in the physiological systems, e.g., antibiotic species (AMO, CLA, ROX, GEN, PC, Cys), amino acid (Cys, Phe, Met, Lys, Tyr, Leu, Try, Thr, AA, Val) and metal ions (Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+) were added into the N-CDs solution and their fluorescence spectra and quenching efficiencies were determined using the same conditions to detect TC samples, which are shown in Fig. 6 . The maximum peak in FL spectra of N-CDs scarcely changed after addition of any types of the substances, which was helpful to calculate the quenching efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6a, the TC exhibited an obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of lignin with a sharp decrease of 70% intensity. However, the N-CDs displayed weak response to most of the other substances. These results demonstrated the N-CDs exhibited remarkable selectivity toward TC.

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism through which the N-CDs visually distinguishing TC was analyzed in detail. The aggregation behavior of N-CDs after addition of TC was evaluated through TEM characterization and the results are found in Fig. S1. It was observed that large particles were not formed in the N-CDs solution after addition of TC. The average sizes of N-CDs after addition of TC were 1.72 nm, which were similar to that 1.71 nm before addition of TC. Therefore, it could be concluded that the N-CDs would not be aggregated after addition of TC. As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorption spectra of N-CDs/TCs mixture exhibited some differences to that of N-CDs and TC, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was probably caused by the static adsorption. The absorption spectrum of TC and the excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs were collected and are shown in Fig. 7a. It was found that the absorption spectrum of TC was well overlapped by the excitation spectrum of the N-CDs and only parts of it was overlapped by the emission spectrum of N-CDs. This result indicated that the fluorescence quenching might be caused by inner filter effect process (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [22,71]. For further confirming the IFE or FRET, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs before and after addition of TC were analyzed (Fig. 7c and d) and recorded to be 7.58 ns and 7.30 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs before and after addition of TC was scarcely changed, confirming the absence of FRET between N-CDs and TC.

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism through which the N-CDs visually distinguishing TC was analyzed in detail. The aggregation behavior of N-CDs after addition of TC was evaluated through TEM characterization and the results are found in Fig. S1. It was observed that large particles were not formed in the N-CDs solution after addition of TC. The average sizes of N-CDs after addition of TC were 1.72 nm, which were similar to that 1.71 nm before addition of TC. Therefore, it could be concluded that the N-CDs would not be aggregated after addition of TC. As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorption spectra of N-CDs/TCs mixture exhibited some differences to that of N-CDs and TC, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was probably caused by the static adsorption. The absorption spectrum of TC and the excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs were collected and are shown in Fig. 7a. It was found that the absorption spectrum of TC was well overlapped by the excitation spectrum of the N-CDs and only parts of it was overlapped by the emission spectrum of N-CDs. This result indicated that the fluorescence quenching might be caused by inner filter effect process (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [22,71]. For further confirming the IFE or FRET, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs before and after addition of TC were analyzed (Fig. 7c and d) and recorded to be 7.58 ns and 7.30 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs before and after addition of TC was scarcely changed, confirming the absence of FRET between N-CDs and TC.

Owing to their excellent fluorescent FL properties, the application of N-CDs as the fluorescent ink was also evaluated by writing some characters on a paper using a pencil filled with the N-CDs solution. The images of the paper under sunlight or UV light irradiation are shown in Fig. 8 . It was observed that the photographs of the hand-written characters on the paper could emit a bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, which was easily seen by the naked eyes. This result confirmed that the as-prepared N-CDs could be an alternative to traditional fluorescent ink. Furthermore, considering their favorable properties, e.g., low-cost, eco-friendliness, facile to be preparing and easily to be utilization, the N-CDs fluorescent ink was potentially applied in the fields of printing with the aim of information storage and encryption.

In summary, a simple, cheap and non-toxicity approach was rationally developed for preparation of N-CDs from CDA via one-pot hydrothermal treatment, among which ammonium hydroxide was employed as the passivation agent. Furthermore, the possible formation process of N-CDs was analyzed. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties with a maximum QY of 22.4%. The fluorescence of N-CDs could be selectively quenched by TC. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of TC in the range of 0–80 μM with a detection limit of 0.06 μM was found. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching was attributed to inner filter effect. The findings in this work not only provided an effective and facile approach to convert waste CDA, present in the discarded cigarette filters, into valuable nanomaterials, but also gave an insight to fabricate feasible sensing platform for detection of TC in biomedical analysis.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
TEM images of the N-CDs before (a) and after (b) addition of TC and size distribution of the N-CDs before (c) and after (d) addition of TC.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.08.243.

All authors have seen the final version of the manuscript. This is the original work of the authors. This manuscript has been submitted exclusively to International Journal of Biological Macromolecules.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1807232), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control (2019KF30), Liaoning Baiqianwan Talents Program and the National Natural Science Foundation (31500493).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Safavi, R. Ahmadi, Z. Mohammadpour, J. Zhou, Fluorescent pH nanosensor based on carbon nanodots for monitoring minor intracellular pH changes, RSC Adv. 6(106) (2016) 104657-104664.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing
ACS Sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, T. Chen, J. J. Gooding, J. Liu, Review of carbon and graphene quantum dots for sensing. ACS Sensors 4(7) (2019) 1732-1748.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor fluorescent graphene quantum dots colorimetrically responsive to all-pH and wide temperature range
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yuan, L. Ding, Y. Li, X. Li, L. Fan, S. Zhou, D. Fang, S. Yang, Multicolor fluorescent graphene quantum dots colorimetrically responsive to all-pH and wide temperature range, Nanoscale 7 (2015) 11727-11733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: pH-responsive fluorescent graphene quantum dots for fluorescence-guided cancer surgery and diagnosis
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Fan, S. Zhou, C. Garcia, L. Fan, J. Zhou, pH-responsive fluorescent graphene quantum dots for fluorescence-guided cancer surgery and diagnosis, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 4928-4933.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile Synthesis of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots with Robust Fluorescence in Strong Alkali and Reversible Fluorescence ‘Off-On’ Switch between Strong Acid and Alkali

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, L. Zhou, Y. Li, R. Deng, H. Zhang, Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with robust fluorescence in strong alkali and reversible fluorescence ‘off-on’ switch between strong acid and alkali, 277 (2018) 373-380.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards pH. Application as nanosensors for a broad range of pH
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Barati, M. Shamsipur, H. Abdollahi, Carbon dots with strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards pH. Application as nanosensors for a broad range of pH, Anal. Chim. Acta. 931 (2016) 25-33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
Sensor. Actuator B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Liu, H. Diao, H. Chang, H. Wang, T. Li, W. Wei, Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging. Sensor. Actuator B-Chem. 241 (2017) 190-198.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lin, H. Zou, T. Yang, Z. Liu, H. Liu, C. Huang, An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers, Nanoscale 8 (2016) 2999-3007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An automated salting-out assisted liquid-liquid microextraction approach using 1-octylamine: on-line separation of tetracycline in urine samples followed by HPLC-UV determination
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Cherkashina, C. Vakh, S. Lebedinets, A. Pochivalov, L. Moskvin, A. Lezov, A. Bulatov, An automated salting-out assisted liquid-liquid microextraction approach using 1-octylamine: on-line separation of tetracycline in urine samples followed by HPLC-UV determination. Talanta 184 (2018) 122-127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A portable synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid based on inner filter effect
Spectrochim. Acta A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, L. Yang, Y. Yuan, S. Pan, J. Yang, J. Yan, H. Zhang, Q. Sun, X. Hu, A portable synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid based on inner filter effect, Spectrochim. Acta A 189 (2018) 139-146.
